caveats: a: No current 423910-specific occupational mix was located.
a: B2B commerce vendor material shows feasible workflows but not realized substitution.
a: Exposure varies widely between institutional team supply, commodity accessories, firearms, pools, fitness equipment, and pleasure craft.
rho: Adoption intention is not implementation.
rho: General distributor evidence may not capture fashion-like variants and returns.
rho: Platform case studies can select digitally mature firms.
e: The industry contains materially different product and regulatory niches.
e: Repeat product orders are less contractually recurring than many service businesses.
e: The injected LMM count is an estimate and includes brand-led or one-off models.
s5: Owner plans do not equal completed transactions.
s5: Sports asset deals include teams, brands, media, and other businesses outside wholesaling.
s5: BizBuySell transactions skew smaller than the target EBITDA band.
q: No direct five-year benefit-retention measure exists.
q: Branded footwear and apparel economics differ from hard goods, team equipment, firearms, pools, and boats.
q: Tariffs and vendor allowances can obscure AI-related retention.
d5: Wholesale-sales growth includes price and mix effects.
d5: Participation does not translate uniformly into equipment purchases.
d5: Category booms such as pickleball can reverse while other categories mature.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Sporting and Recreational Goods and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Product gross margin on bulk and seasonal B2B orders, retailer replenishment, institutional contracts, and specialty dealer supply, with negotiated price lists, credit terms, freight, multi-location fulfillment, returns, warranty, parts, and occasional repair or service agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers and brand owners whose economics are primarily product IP, direct-to-consumer retailers, captive sales branches, brokers, one-off marine pleasure-craft or major-equipment sellers without repeat supply activity, financing vehicles, and nontransferable owner-personal operations.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market sporting and recreational goods wholesalers serving external retailers, teams, schools, clubs, gyms, institutions, facilities, and specialty dealers through repeat or seasonal replenishment, bulk orders, account-specific catalogs, parts, warranty, and fulfillment support.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts cover a broader durable-goods wholesale grouping, while digital-workflow evidence describes B2B sporting goods and distributors generally rather than wage-weighted tasks in 423910. I map sales, customer service, office, catalog, pricing, purchasing, forecasting, returns, and inventory work to AI exposure, while assigning limited substitution to warehouse handling, equipment assembly, repairs, samples, demonstrations, relationship selling, and physical logistics. Sporting-goods firms may have more marketing and seasonal-planning work than the broad proxy, biasing exposure downward.
caveats: No current 423910-specific occupational mix was located.
B2B commerce vendor material shows feasible workflows but not realized substitution.
Exposure varies widely between institutional team supply, commodity accessories, firearms, pools, fitness equipment, and pleasure craft.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted occupation extract plus time studies across inside sales, account management, purchasing, merchandising, marketing, customer service, warehouse, returns, warranty, and finance, paired with order-channel and ERP touch data.
a.rationale: Large catalogs, seasonal orders, customer-specific price lists, emailed purchase orders, inventory allocation, product content, returns, and frequent retailer communication create meaningful digital work. AI can automate document intake, SKU matching, forecast support, catalog enrichment, account outreach, and routine service. Physical fulfillment and relationship-intensive category selling remain less exposed.

d5.bridge: SFIA wholesale sales and participation measure the broader sporting-goods ecosystem, including apparel, footwear, manufacturers, and retail channels, not constant-quality demand for the current 423910 distributor service basket. I translate sustained participation and sales growth into modest positive end demand, then discount nominal price/tariff effects, post-pandemic normalization, category fads, and channel migration. The broad SFIA market may overstate demand for this NAICS's hard-goods mix.
caveats: Wholesale-sales growth includes price and mix effects.
Participation does not translate uniformly into equipment purchases.
Category booms such as pickleball can reverse while other categories mature.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 423910 Census sales and product-line quantities, institutional and retail sell-through, participation by category, replacement cycles, inventory-to-sales ratios, and channel shares through 2031.
d5.rationale: SFIA reports 250 million U.S. participants in 2025, up 1.2% year over year, and sporting-goods wholesale sales of nearly $130 billion, up 3.7% in 2025 and 34.7% since 2020. These figures support durable product use, but constant-price five-year demand is likely slower than nominal sales and uneven across mature and fast-growing activities.

caveats: The industry contains materially different product and regulatory niches.
Repeat product orders are less contractually recurring than many service businesses.
The injected LMM count is an estimate and includes brand-led or one-off models.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level classification of 423910 LMM businesses by customer type, reorder frequency, contracted or scheduled replenishment, parts/warranty/service revenue, DTC share, whole-equipment sales, brand concentration, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: The code covers sporting goods, billiards, firearms and ammunition, marine pleasure craft and supplies, pools, bicycles, exercise equipment, playground equipment, and related categories. Repeat retailer replenishment and institutional team, school, gym, and facility supply can meet the lens; firms dominated by one-time boats, pools, major equipment, brand representation, or spot seasonal orders may not. No source measures the LMM firm share with recurring outsourced-service and transferability characteristics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423910 ($5,054,101,192) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423910 ($72,273,962,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,007,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423910); class <5: 2,769 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,475,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $167,738 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 753 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,742,066 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $425,473 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 482 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,429,693 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,072,156 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 445 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,585,337 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,818,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 129 firms, avg revenue/firm $153,825,922 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,490,007 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $413,286,365 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,990,660 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 927

o.bridge: Sporting-goods channel evidence shows both direct-to-consumer expansion and renewed brand use of wholesale, but does not measure the share of year-five quantity requiring a 423910-style accountable merchant wholesaler. I allocate continued operator need to credit, assortment aggregation, institutional sales, bulk freight, local inventory, returns, warranty, parts, and multi-location fulfillment, while allowing branded products and standardized accessories to move direct or through marketplaces. Retail e-commerce statistics may overstate disintermediation for teams and institutions.
caveats: A warehouse or platform operator may remain necessary even when the independent wholesaler is bypassed.
Institutional and specialty-dealer channels are more durable than consumer-branded goods.
Channel strategies can reverse, as recent wholesale resurgence demonstrates.
o.change_evidence: Brand and retailer shipment data showing shares sold through independent wholesalers, manufacturer direct programs, marketplaces, buying groups, and DTC; customer interviews on which assortment, credit, logistics, warranty, and service functions require an independent operator.
o.rationale: Physical goods still need ownership, aggregation, fulfillment, credit, returns, and warranty accountability, and some brands have returned emphasis to wholesale for reach. Yet roughly 30% of global sports and recreation purchases already occur online, brands can integrate DTC and wholesale in one back end, and B2B buyers can self-serve catalogs and replenishment. Much quantity can therefore persist without an independent wholesaler of the current kind.

q.bridge: Sporting-goods commerce sources document online price transparency, channel shifts, retailer self-service, and wholesale resurgence rather than the discounted share of AI benefit retained by 423910 operators. I infer substantial pass-through because many products are branded and comparable, retailers can switch suppliers, and brands can sell direct; negotiated catalogs, exclusivity, credit, assortment, and service preserve some capture. Consumer-facing e-commerce data may overstate substitution in institutional B2B accounts.
caveats: No direct five-year benefit-retention measure exists.
Branded footwear and apparel economics differ from hard goods, team equipment, firearms, pools, and boats.
Tariffs and vendor allowances can obscure AI-related retention.
q.change_evidence: Account-level gross-margin, discount, rebate, freight, churn, and renewal data before and after automation, split by institutional versus retailer accounts, exclusive versus widely available brands, contract versus spot orders, and DTC exposure.
q.rationale: AI savings in ordering, merchandising, forecasting, and service are vulnerable to price competition and buyer expectations for faster digital service. A cited B2B survey found 75% of buyers would switch for a smoother web-store experience. Retention is stronger where the wholesaler has exclusive lines, scarce inventory, institutional contracts, customization, multi-location fulfillment, and warranty or parts support.

rho.bridge: General distributor surveys provide adoption and impact rates, and sporting-goods commerce evidence shows digital ordering and catalog capabilities; neither measures the five-year implemented share in LMM 423910 firms. I discount for fragmented product data, seasonality, legacy ERP, brand restrictions, channel conflict, size/color variants, returns, implementation cost, and change capacity. Technology-oriented survey and platform users likely overstate readiness.
caveats: Adoption intention is not implementation.
General distributor evidence may not capture fashion-like variants and returns.
Platform case studies can select digitally mature firms.
rho.change_evidence: A 2026-2031 panel of sporting-goods wholesalers measuring touchless order share, catalog-match and forecast accuracy, automated customer-service resolution, return exceptions, implementation cost, labor hours saved, and avoided hiring by ERP and channel mix.
rho.rationale: The relevant workflows are standard enough for embedded ERP and commerce tools, but measured distributor impact remains early: 29% reported AI use, roughly 10% positive impact, and more than 43% intended adoption within two years in one survey. Five years supports wider rollout, though smaller specialty wholesalers will retain manual exceptions and relationship work.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned sales or transfers among all U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers in 423910. I start below the reported 22% five-year figure because brand dependence, inventory risk, seasonality, customer concentration, and direct-channel erosion can impair transferability, while observed sports-sector investor activity and the broader wholesale transaction market support a nontrivial rate. Public sports deals skew far larger and more brand-oriented than the lens.
caveats: Owner plans do not equal completed transactions.
Sports asset deals include teams, brands, media, and other businesses outside wholesaling.
BizBuySell transactions skew smaller than the target EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: Closed 423910 control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, SBA change-of-ownership loans, sporting-goods brokers, and strategic buyer disclosures, with a denominator of eligible firms and flags for inventory-only sales, recapitalizations, and brand/manufacturer deals.
s5.rationale: Owner demographics provide potential supply and wholesale businesses demonstrably trade. Sporting-goods investor interest is active, but a distributor's value can evaporate if key brand rights, retailer relationships, or trend-sensitive inventory do not transfer. Strong institutional accounts, diversified categories, and clean inventory improve completion odds.

principal_driver: Catalog-heavy recurring and seasonal B2B workflows are AI-addressable against a broadly durable participation-driven demand base.
principal_weakness: Independent-wholesaler necessity and benefit retention are vulnerable to brand-direct commerce, marketplaces, and highly transparent product pricing.
